description: Alerting rules for Apache Spark on Amazon EMR — executor health, stage performance, shuffle, GC, and S3 access

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — Executor Out of Memory
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# Executor Out of Memory

A Spark executor threw `java.lang.OutOfMemoryError`. The container will be killed by YARN shortly.

## Triage
1. Check which executor and host: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.component_short == "Executor" AND log.level == "ERROR" | KEEP @timestamp, spark.executor.id, spark.task.host, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 10`
2. Check GC pressure leading up to the OOM: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.gc.time_ms IS NOT NULL | STATS avg_gc = AVG(spark.gc.time_ms) BY ts = BUCKET(@timestamp, 50, ?_tstart, ?_tend)`
3. Check if the same executor keeps failing (bad node vs bad data)

## Resolution
- Increase `spark.executor.memory` or `spark.executor.memoryOverhead`
- Check for data skew — one partition may be much larger than others
- Enable adaptive query execution: `spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true`
- If recurring on one node, check node health in YARN

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — Executor Lost (Heartbeat Timeout)
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# Executor Lost

Multiple executors lost contact with the driver. This causes task re-computation and can cascade into job failure.

## Triage
1. Check which executors and hosts: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.executor.loss_reason IS NOT NULL | KEEP @timestamp, spark.executor.id, spark.executor.host, spark.executor.loss_reason | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 20`
2. Check if losses cluster on one host (hardware issue) or are spread across the cluster (network/GC)
3. Look for YARN container kill events (exit 137 = OOM killed, exit 143 = SIGTERM)

## Resolution
- If OOM-killed: increase executor memory or reduce parallelism
- If network timeout: check `spark.network.timeout` and security group rules
- If on Spot instances: check for Spot interruptions in CloudTrail
- If on one host: consider decommissioning the node

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — Stage Failure (Job Aborted)
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# Stage Failure — Job Aborted

The DAGScheduler aborted a job because a stage failed too many times. This is typically the final failure after multiple task retries.

## Triage
1. Find which stage failed: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.component_short == "DAGScheduler" AND log.level == "ERROR" | KEEP @timestamp, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 5`
2. Check task-level failures: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.component_short == "TaskSetManager" AND log.level == "WARN" | KEEP @timestamp, spark.task.id, spark.task.executor_id, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 20`
3. Look for the root cause in executor errors (OOM, FetchFailed, S3 access denied)

## Resolution
- If FetchFailed: likely a shuffle issue — increase `spark.shuffle.io.maxRetries`
- If executor OOM: increase memory or check for skewed partitions
- If S3 errors: check IAM roles and bucket policies
- Consider enabling speculative execution: `spark.speculation=true`

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — Shuffle Fetch Failures
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# Shuffle Fetch Failures

Executors are failing to fetch shuffle blocks from other executors. This causes task retries and can lead to stage/job failure.

## Triage
1. Check shuffle volume: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.shuffle.write_bytes IS NOT NULL | STATS total_write = SUM(spark.shuffle.write_bytes), total_read = SUM(spark.shuffle.read_bytes)`
2. Check if failures are from specific executors: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE message LIKE "*FetchFailed*" | KEEP @timestamp, spark.task.executor_id, spark.shuffle.id, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 20`
3. Look for executor losses around the same time

## Resolution
- Increase `spark.shuffle.io.maxRetries` and `spark.shuffle.io.retryWait`
- If disk-bound: increase `spark.local.dir` to use multiple disks
- If network-bound: check for network saturation between nodes
- Reduce shuffle by repartitioning: `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`
- Enable external shuffle service: `spark.shuffle.service.enabled=true`

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — High GC Pressure
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# High GC Pressure

Spark executors are spending over 30 seconds in garbage collection. This is an early warning before OOM failures.

## Triage
1. Check GC trends: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.gc.time_ms IS NOT NULL | STATS avg_gc = AVG(spark.gc.time_ms), max_gc = MAX(spark.gc.time_ms) BY ts = BUCKET(@timestamp, 50, ?_tstart, ?_tend)`
2. Check memory usage on the Spark dashboard
3. Look for which application is causing pressure: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.gc.time_ms > 20000 | STATS count = COUNT() BY spark.app.name | SORT count DESC`

## Resolution
- Increase `spark.executor.memory` (heap) and `spark.executor.memoryOverhead` (off-heap)
- Switch to G1GC: `-XX:+UseG1GC` in `spark.executor.extraJavaOptions`
- Reduce in-memory caching: `.unpersist()` DataFrames when no longer needed
- Check for broadcast joins on large tables — disable with `spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold=-1`

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — S3 Access Denied
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# S3 Access Denied

Spark cannot read or write S3 data due to permissions. The job will fail if the data is required.

## Triage
1. Find the S3 path: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.component_short == "S3AFileSystem" AND log.level == "ERROR" | KEEP @timestamp, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 10`
2. Check the EMR cluster's IAM instance profile and EC2 role
3. Cross-reference with CloudTrail S3 events for the denied call

## Resolution
- Update the EMR instance profile IAM policy to include the S3 bucket
- Check for S3 bucket policies that deny cross-account access
- If using EMRFS: verify the EMRFS role mappings in security configuration
- Check for S3 VPC endpoint policies if in a private subnet

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — Abnormally Slow Stages
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# Abnormally Slow Stages

A Spark stage took over 5 minutes to complete. This often indicates data skew, insufficient parallelism, or resource contention.

## Triage
1. Find which stages are slow: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.stage.duration_s > 300 | KEEP @timestamp, spark.stage.id, spark.stage.name, spark.stage.duration_s | SORT spark.stage.duration_s DESC | LIMIT 10`
2. Check task duration spread — a few long tasks indicate skew: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.task.duration_ms IS NOT NULL | STATS p50 = PERCENTILE(spark.task.duration_ms, 50), p99 = PERCENTILE(spark.task.duration_ms, 99)`
3. If p99 >> p50, you have data skew

## Resolution
- Enable AQE: `spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true` and `spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true`
- Repartition skewed data: `.repartition(col("skewed_key"))`
- Add salt keys for skewed joins
- Increase `spark.sql.shuffle.partitions` if stages have too few tasks

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — YARN Container Killed

params.esQuery: {"query":{"bool":{"filter":[{"term":{"event.dataset":"aws.emr_logs"}},{"bool":{"should":[{"match_phrase":{"message":"Exit status: 137"}},{"match_phrase":{"message":"Container killed on request"}},{"match_phrase":{"message":"Container from a bad node"}}],"minimum_should_match":1}}]}}}

relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# YARN Container Killed

YARN is killing Spark executor containers. Exit code 137 means the container was OOM-killed by the OS; exit 143 means graceful termination.

## Triage
1. Check which containers and hosts: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE message LIKE "*Exit status*" OR message LIKE "*Container killed*" | KEEP @timestamp, spark.executor.id, spark.executor.host, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 20`
2. Check YARN memory utilization on the EMR dashboard
3. Look at `yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb` vs actual usage

## Resolution
- Exit 137 (OOM): increase `spark.executor.memoryOverhead` (YARN kills when total exceeds allocated)
- Exit 143 (SIGTERM): usually from cluster scaling or preemption — check auto-scaling events
- If on one host: the node may have hardware issues
- Consider enabling `spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true` with proper min/max executors

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — Application Failed
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# Spark Application Failed

A Spark application ended with a failure status. This is the top-level failure event.

## Triage
1. Find the application: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.event_type IS NOT NULL | KEEP @timestamp, spark.app.id, spark.app.name, spark.event_type, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 20`
2. Work backwards through the failure chain: application → job → stage → task
3. Check the Spark dashboard for the timeline of events

## Resolution
- Identify the root cause from the stage/task failure alerts
- Check if this is a recurring failure (same app, same time of day)
- If the app was killed externally: check EMR step status and YARN app status
- Consider setting `spark.task.maxFailures` higher for transient issues

rules.name: [CloudLoadGen] Spark — S3 Slow Operations
relatedDashboards: Amazon EMR — Spark Driver & Executor Logs
rules.investigationGuide:
# S3 Slow Operations

The S3A filesystem client is logging slow operations. This can cause stages to take much longer than expected.

## Triage
1. Check which S3 paths are slow: `FROM logs-aws.emr_logs* | WHERE spark.component_short == "S3AFileSystem" AND log.level == "WARN" | KEEP @timestamp, message | SORT @timestamp DESC | LIMIT 20`
2. Check if this correlates with high S3 request rates (throttling)
3. Compare with S3 CloudWatch metrics for the bucket

## Resolution
- If throttled: add randomised prefixes to S3 keys to spread across partitions
- Enable S3A fast upload: `fs.s3a.fast.upload=true`
- Use committer v2: `spark.hadoop.mapreduce.fileoutputcommitter.algorithm.version=2`
- Consider S3 Express One Zone for latency-sensitive workloads
